verses.text: Zedekiah's rebellion brings Nebuchadnezzar to besiege Jerusalem; the siege drags on until famine grips the city and there is no bread for the people. The end is slow and bitter. Sin's reckoning, when it finally comes, can be a long agony.

Zedekiah's rebellion brings Nebuchadnezzar to besiege Jerusalem; the siege drags on until famine grips the city and there is no bread for the people. The end is slow and bitter. Sin's reckoning, when it finally comes, can be a long agony.

Zedekiah's rebellion brings Nebuchadnezzar to besiege Jerusalem; the siege drags on until famine grips the city and there is no bread for the people. The end is slow and bitter. Sin's reckoning, when it finally comes, can be a long agony.

The wall is breached; Zedekiah flees but is caught, made to watch his sons killed, then blinded and bound for Babylon. The last thing he sees is his sons' death. The king who would not see by faith ends his days in literal darkness.

Nebuzaradan burns the house of the LORD, the palace, and every great house, and breaks down Jerusalem's walls. Solomon's glorious temple goes up in flames. The place God filled with His glory is left a ruin, as the prophets long had warned.

Nebuzaradan burns the house of the LORD, the palace, and every great house, and breaks down Jerusalem's walls. Solomon's glorious temple goes up in flames. The place God filled with His glory is left a ruin, as the prophets long had warned.

Nebuzaradan burns the house of the LORD, the palace, and every great house, and breaks down Jerusalem's walls. Solomon's glorious temple goes up in flames. The place God filled with His glory is left a ruin, as the prophets long had warned.

The rest of the people are carried to Babylon, only the poorest left to tend the land. The nation is emptied of its strength. What God gave as an inheritance is lost through the long refusal to obey Him.

The rest of the people are carried to Babylon, only the poorest left to tend the land. The nation is emptied of its strength. What God gave as an inheritance is lost through the long refusal to obey Him.

The Babylonians break up and carry off all the bronze of the temple — the pillars, the sea, the vessels — too heavy to weigh. The sacred furnishings become spoil. Everything Israel trusted in but did not honour is stripped away.

The chief priests and city officials are taken to Riblah and executed; 'so Judah was carried away out of their land.' The leaders fall with the people. The long story of the kingdom ends in exile, exactly as God had said it would.

Gedaliah is left as governor and urges submission and peace, but he is murdered by Ishmael, and the frightened remnant flees to Egypt. Even the small mercy of a wise governor is thrown away. A people bent on their own way refuse the very peace God offers.

Years later, Babylon's new king frees Jehoiachin from prison, speaks kindly, and gives him a seat above other kings and a daily portion all his life. The book ends not in total darkness but a flicker of hope. The line of David lives on, and with it the promise of a King to come.
